ruleset.name: Design Rules
ruleset.description: The Design Ruleset contains a collection of rules that find software design related problems.

rule.name: ExitExpression
rule.description: An exit-expression within regular code is untestable and therefore it should be avoided. Consider moving the exit-expression into some kind of startup script where an error/exception code is returned to the calling environment. In C# this means calls to Environment.Exit() or Environment.FailFast().

rule.name: GotoStatement
rule.description: Goto makes code harder to read and it is nearly impossible to understand the control flow of an application that uses this language construct. Therefore it should be avoided. Consider replacing goto with regular control structures and separate methods, which are easier to read. Note: goto case and goto default inside switch statements are idiomatic C# and are NOT flagged by this rule.

rule.name: CountInLoopExpression
rule.description: Using .Count(), .Count, or .Length in loop conditions is considered bad practice and is a potential source of bugs, especially when the loop manipulates a collection, as the count is re-evaluated on each iteration.

rule.name: DevelopmentCodeFragment
rule.message: The {0} {1}() calls the typical debug function {2}() which is mostly only used during development.
rule.description: Functions like Console.WriteLine, Debug.WriteLine, Debugger.Break, etc. are normally only used during development and therefore such calls in production code are a good indicator that they were just forgotten.

property.name: unwanted-functions
property.description: Comma-separated list of additional suspect function calls (e.g. MyLogger.Debug,Trace.Write).

rule.name: EmptyCatchBlock
rule.message: Avoid using empty catch blocks in {0}.
rule.description: Usually empty catch blocks are a bad idea because you are silently swallowing an error condition and then continuing execution. Occasionally this may be the right thing to do, but often it is a sign that a developer saw an exception, did not know what to do about it, and so used an empty catch to silence the problem.

rule.name: CouplingBetweenObjects
rule.message: The class {0} has a coupling between objects value of {1}. Consider to reduce the number of dependencies under {2}.
rule.description: A class with too many dependencies has negative impacts on several quality aspects of a class. This includes quality criteria like stability, maintainability and understandability. The coupling is measured by counting distinct non-builtin type names referenced in fields, method parameters, return types, and object creations.

rule.name: GlobalVariable
rule.description:
ADAPTED: Flags non-readonly, non-const static fields that are actually mutated somewhere in the class (assigned, incremented, or passed ref/out). Mutable static state hurts testability and is unsafe under concurrency.
By default only fields that are actually mutated are reported. Set the `report-immutable` property to also flag un-mutated mutable statics.
`static readonly` and `const` fields are never flagged.

property.name: report-immutable
property.description: Also flag mutable static fields that are never mutated in the file.

rule.name: LackOfCohesionOfMethods
rule.message: The {0} has a Lack of Cohesion Of Methods (LCOM4) value of {1}. Consider to split this class into {1} smaller classes.
rule.description:
Computes the LCOM4 cohesion metric per class. Methods are linked when they share a field or when one calls another via `this` or unqualified. The metric is the number of disconnected method groups; a value above `maximum` (default 1) means the class bundles unrelated responsibilities and is a candidate for splitting.
Trivial getters and setters are excluded from the graph; a call to a getter counts as use of its backing field. Stateless methods (no field use, no sibling calls) are ignored so data carriers with accessors are not falsely flagged.

property.name: maximum
property.description: The maximum number of allowed LCOM4 components.
